pattern: The cost of inserting a value into a growing sorted array is the smaller of how many already-placed values are less than it and how many are greater, and a Fenwick tree over the value range lets you answer both counts in logarithmic time as you insert one value at a time.
hints: You never need to actually build or shift a sorted array -- for each incoming value, all you need are two counts among the values already inserted: how many are strictly smaller, and how many are strictly greater.

A Fenwick tree (binary indexed tree) indexed by value lets you get 'how many inserted values are <= v' in logarithmic time, from which both the smaller-count and the greater-count fall out immediately.

Process the values one at a time in the given order: query the tree for counts before inserting the current value, add min(smaller, greater) to a running total, then insert the value into the tree and move on -- remember the total can be large, so return it modulo 1,000,000,007.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, mixed ascending and descending run
strictly increasing sequence, every insert costs nothing
strictly decreasing sequence, every insert costs the full count so far
every value identical, all counted as neither strictly smaller nor greater
values at the extreme boundaries of the allowed range

stress.description: random values across a moderate range and length
large sequence at the real constraint size with a narrow value range to force many ties

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def createSortedArray(self, instructions):
        import bisect
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        arr = []
        cost = 0
        for v in instructions:
            less = bisect.bisect_left(arr, v)
            greater = len(arr) - bisect.bisect_right(arr, v)
            cost += min(less, greater)
            bisect.insort(arr, v)
        return cost % MOD
